questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for C. difficile. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The nurse receives the result just before transferring the client to a semi-private room.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit has gowns, gloves, soap-and-water handwashing sinks, and sporicidal disinfectant available.

options.text: Initiate contact-plus-enteric precautions with gown and gloves
Place the client in an appropriate room and dedicate equipment when possible
Perform soap-and-water hand hygiene after care
Ensure the environment is cleaned with a sporicidal product

questions.rationale: C. difficile prevention works as a bundle: start precautions, protect room placement and equipment, use soap-and-water hand hygiene, and ensure sporicidal cleaning. The sequence keeps spores from spreading while also protecting the next patient environment.
questions.takeaway: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.
questions.speedCue: The shortcut is spores: alcohol gel alone is not enough.

visualRationale.title: C_diff_transmission_prevention_bundle
visualRationale.caption: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.
visualRationale.conclusion: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.

questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Place the nursing actions in the order that best protects the client before the first enteral feeding starts.
questions.scenario: A newly inserted small-bore feeding tube is in place, and the provider has written the order to begin feedings.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is preparing the bedside setup while waiting for placement confirmation.

options.text: Confirm the prescribed placement-verification method has been completed
Position the client with the head of bed elevated
Flush the tube per protocol after confirmation
Begin the feeding only after placement and position are safe

questions.rationale: Before the first feeding, the nurse verifies safe placement, protects aspiration risk with positioning, flushes per protocol, and only then starts the feeding. Starting nutrition before location is confirmed creates avoidable airway harm.
questions.takeaway: The first enteral feed starts only after safe placement is confirmed.
questions.speedCue: Placement before pouring formula is the shortcut.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client has been declared brain-dead and the family asks about organ donation. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The family is waiting at the bedside, and physiologic support is still running.
questions.additionalInfo: The facility follows an organ-procurement-notification policy.

options.text: Notify the organ procurement organization per policy
Maintain prescribed physiologic support while coordination occurs
Provide the family with calm support and accurate process information
Document the notification and ongoing coordination steps

questions.rationale: Once brain death is established, the nurse follows policy by notifying the organ procurement team, maintaining physiologic support, supporting the family, and documenting the process. The sequence matters because donation coordination depends on both timely notification and ongoing stability of organ perfusion.
questions.takeaway: Donation questions after brain death should trigger policy-based notification and physiologic support, not improvisation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each arterial blood gas pattern, click to specify whether the pattern is primarily respiratory or primarily metabolic.
questions.scenario: The nurse is triaging several ABG reports and needs to sort the primary problem quickly before acting.
questions.additionalInfo: Each row represents the main disturbance, not the final compensation story.

options.text: Primarily respiratory
Primarily metabolic

matrixRows.label: pH 7.29, PaCO2 58 mm Hg, HCO3 26 mEq/L
pH 7.30, PaCO2 31 mm Hg, HCO3 16 mEq/L
pH 7.50, PaCO2 30 mm Hg, HCO3 24 mEq/L
pH 7.48, PaCO2 42 mm Hg, HCO3 31 mEq/L

questions.rationale: Fast ABG interpretation starts by deciding which value is pushing the pH out of range. Elevated or low carbon dioxide points toward respiratory causes, while an abnormal bicarbonate driving the pH change points toward metabolic causes.

distractorRationales.a: The clinical error in Primarily respiratory is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by while an abnormal bicarbonate driving the pH change points toward metabolic causes. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.b: The problem with Primarily metabolic is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, although the nurse should be guided by Fast ABG interpretation starts by deciding which value is pushing the pH out of range. That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Find what is moving the pH first; compensation is the second step.
questions.speedCue: pH first, then match the value pushing it.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each postoperative task, click to specify whether the task should stay with the RN or can be delegated to the UAP.
questions.scenario: The RN is caring for several postoperative clients and is working with an experienced UAP.
questions.additionalInfo: The RN must decide which work can safely move and which work must stay with licensed nursing judgment.

options.text: Keep with RN
Delegate to UAP

matrixRows.label: Assess a client with new shortness of breath two hours after surgery
Assist a stable client to the bathroom for the first time after the RN has assessed
Reinforce coughing and deep-breathing after the RN provides the initial teaching
Evaluate a new report of uncontrolled incisional pain

questions.rationale: Delegation becomes faster when the nurse separates stable, predictable tasks from new assessment or evaluation needs. Acute respiratory change and uncontrolled pain require RN judgment, while routine assistance and reinforcement after assessment can move safely to the UAP.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with Keep with RN is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, while the decisive cue is Acute respiratory change and uncontrolled pain require RN judgment. The safer answer is the row-specific answer pattern because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.b: Delegate to UAP fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on Delegation becomes faster when the nurse separates stable, predictable tasks from new assessment or evaluation needs. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.

questions.takeaway: New problems stay with the RN; stable, routine assistance can move.
questions.speedCue: Ask whether the task needs fresh assessment or evaluation. If yes, it stays with the RN.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when this client becomes increasingly paranoid and agitated?
questions.scenario: A client with schizophrenia is pacing, shouting that staff members are trying to poison him, and clenching his fists.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit is noisy, and several other clients have gathered nearby to watch the scene.

options.text: This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal of excess triggers
The nurse should argue with the delusion to prove the belief is false
The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction
The nurse should leave immediately without setting limits or assessing risk

questions.rationale: Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation, and calm limit setting rather than confrontation. Arguing about the delusion or adding more external stimulation increases the risk that the client's distress escalates into violence.

distractorRationales.b: This option makes The nurse should argue with the delusion to prove the belief is false the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so It is less defensible because This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.c: The clinical error in The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction is that it normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, without accounting for Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation. It is less defensible because This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats The nurse should leave immediately without setting limits or assessing risk as the key issue, although the nurse should be guided by In psychosis, the nurse leads with safety and calm structure, not debate. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The nurse should prioritize This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal; otherwise clinical safety risk remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: In psychosis, the nurse leads with safety and calm structure, not debate.
questions.speedCue: Paranoia plus clenched fists means lower the room before you lower the delusion.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when family demands conflict with a documented client directive?
questions.scenario: A client with metastatic cancer has a signed advance directive declining intubation and CPR.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is now unresponsive, and two adult children insist the nurse should 'do everything' while the provider is en route.

options.text: Honor the documented advance directive while escalating the conflict through the provider and ethics chain
Ignore the directive because family requests automatically override prior written wishes
Delay all action until the family agrees on a plan
Ask the family to sign a new code-status form at the bedside before doing anything else

questions.rationale: A valid advance directive reflects the client's expressed wishes and should guide care when the client can no longer speak. The nurse should follow that documented plan while escalating the family conflict through the proper chain rather than treating family pressure as a replacement for the client's autonomy.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting Ignore the directive because family requests automatically override prior written wishes would be unsafe, although the nurse should be guided by advance directive conflict family safety. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so The safer answer is Honor the documented advance directive while escalating the conflict through the provider and ethics chain because delaying circulation and perfusion support can worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Delay all action until the family agrees on a plan, and misses that advance directive conflict family safety. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The correct path is Honor the documented advance directive while escalating the conflict through the provider and ethics chain, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Ask the family to sign a new code-status form at the bedside before doing anything else is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, but the stem cue is advance directive conflict family safety. The correct path is Honor the documented advance directive while escalating the conflict through the provider and ethics chain, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: When family demands conflict with a valid directive, the client's documented wishes stay at the center of care.
questions.speedCue: The shortcut is autonomy first: a signed directive is the client's voice when the family is distressed.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move after this abnormal gestational-diabetes screen?
questions.scenario: A pregnant client at 28 weeks has an abnormal glucose screening result and says, 'I only feel thirsty because I'm pregnant.'
questions.additionalInfo: The client asks whether anything really needs to change if the baby is moving normally.

options.text: This result needs follow-up teaching and blood-glucose management planning because untreated hyperglycemia raises maternal and fetal risk
The result is expected in pregnancy and does not need any follow-up unless contractions begin
The client should stop all carbohydrates immediately until the next prenatal visit
Fetal movement alone rules out meaningful glucose-related risk

questions.rationale: An abnormal gestational-diabetes screen needs follow-up and counseling because hyperglycemia affects both maternal and fetal outcomes. The nurse should frame this as ongoing health-promotion and risk-reduction work, not as a benign pregnancy symptom pattern.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on The result is expected in pregnancy and does not need any follow-up unless contractions begin, despite the clinical warning in A pregnant client at 28 weeks has an abnormal glucose screening result and says, 'I only feel thirsty. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so The nurse should prioritize This result needs follow-up teaching and blood-glucose management planning because untreated hyperglycemia raises maternal and fetal; otherwise unstable glucose physiology remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats The client should stop all carbohydrates immediately until the next prenatal visit as the key issue, rather than acting on A pregnant client at 28 weeks has an abnormal glucose screening result and says, 'I only feel thirsty. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of unstable glucose physiology and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: Fetal movement alone rules out meaningful glucose-related risk fits a lower-risk interpretation, while the decisive cue is A pregnant client at 28 weeks has an abnormal glucose screening result and says, 'I only feel thirsty. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize This result needs follow-up teaching and blood-glucose management planning because untreated hyperglycemia raises maternal and fetal; otherwise unstable glucose physiology remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: An abnormal gestational-diabetes screen is a management issue, not a reassurance cue.
questions.speedCue: Normal fetal movement does not cancel a maternal glucose problem.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority after recognizing that the wrong dose of insulin was given?
questions.scenario: The nurse discovers that 10 units of rapid-acting insulin were administered instead of the prescribed 4 units.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is awake but now diaphoretic and shaky, and the blood glucose result is pending.

options.text: Treat the client as the priority, assess and intervene for hypoglycemia, notify the provider, then complete the reporting chain
Complete the incident report before assessing the client so the timeline is accurate
Wait for symptoms to worsen so the provider has clear evidence of harm
Ask the next nurse to decide whether the error needs to be disclosed

questions.rationale: After a medication error, the unstable client comes before paperwork. The nurse should assess and treat the potential hypoglycemia first, notify the provider, and then complete the required reporting process rather than allowing documentation to delay urgent care.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting Complete the incident report before assessing the client so the timeline is accurate would be unsafe, despite the clinical warning in The nurse discovers that 10 units of rapid-acting insulin were administered instead of the prescribed 4 units. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Treat the client as the priority, assess and intervene for hypoglycemia, notify the provider, then complete, not a step that delays stabilization of unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: Selecting Wait for symptoms to worsen so the provider has clear evidence of harm would be unsafe, although the nurse should be guided by The nurse discovers that 10 units of rapid-acting insulin were administered instead of the prescribed 4 units. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Choosing it can delay glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization and leave unstable glucose physiology unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Ask the next nurse to decide whether the error needs to be disclosed is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, but the stem cue is the blood glucose result is pending. Treat the client as the priority, assess and intervene for hypoglycemia, notify the provider, then complete better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Reporting matters, but the harmed or unstable client always comes first.
questions.speedCue: Error chain shortcut: patient first, provider second, paperwork third.

questions.stem: A client with limited mobility is admitted from home and has blanchable redness over the sacrum. Which action should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Turn and reposition the client on a regular schedule.
Massage the reddened area to increase circulation.
Place a heating pad under the sacrum.
Keep the client flat to avoid friction.

questions.rationale: Blanchable redness indicates pressure-related skin stress that may be reversed if offloading begins early. The first nursing intervention is to relieve pressure and reposition regularly. Massage and heat can worsen tissue injury, and keeping the client flat increases sustained pressure over the sacrum.

distractorRationales.b: This option makes Massage the reddened area to increase circulation the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by Early pressure injury prevention starts with offloading and routine repositioning. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Turn and reposition the client on a regular schedule better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: Place a heating pad under the sacrum would not address the priority, and misses that Early pressure injury prevention starts with offloading and routine repositioning. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for circulation and perfusion support while circulation or perfusion progresses.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Keep the client flat to avoid friction is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for Massage and heat can worsen tissue injury. It is less defensible because Turn and reposition the client on a regular schedule targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first. Nursing safety depends on matching the option to the client's active clinical risk.

questions.takeaway: Early pressure injury prevention starts with offloading and routine repositioning.

visualRationale.caption: Early pressure injury prevention starts with offloading and routine repositioning.
visualRationale.conclusion: Early pressure injury prevention starts with offloading and routine repositioning.

questions.stem: A confused hospitalized client repeatedly tries to pull out the IV line. Which nursing action should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Use a less restrictive alternative such as reorientation and closer observation.
Apply wrist restraints immediately and obtain the order later.
Sedate the client so the client will sleep through the shift.
Ignore the behavior because the line can be restarted if needed.

questions.rationale: The least restrictive intervention should be tried first when a client is at risk for pulling at equipment. Reorientation, family presence, diversion, or closer observation may solve the problem without restraint-related harm. Restraints and sedatives carry risk and require clear indication, monitoring, and policy-based use.

distractorRationales.b: Apply wrist restraints immediately and obtain the order later fits a lower-risk interpretation, while the decisive cue is The least restrictive intervention should be tried first when a client is at risk for pulling at equipment. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The priority is Use a less restrictive alternative such as reorientation and closer observation, not a step that delays stabilization of clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Sedate the client so the client will sleep through the shift, when the bedside priority is driven by Try the least restrictive safety intervention first before escalating to restraints. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so It is less defensible because Use a less restrictive alternative such as reorientation and closer observation targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Ignore the behavior because the line can be restarted if needed as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in Reorientation, family presence, diversion, or closer observation may solve the problem without restraint-related harm. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The safer answer is Use a less restrictive alternative such as reorientation and closer observation because delaying bedside safety action can worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Try the least restrictive safety intervention first before escalating to restraints.

questions.stem: A client treated for pneumonia becomes confused, has a temperature of 102.2 F (39 C), blood pressure 86/50 mmHg, heart rate 124/min, and lactate 4.2 mmol/L. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Activate the sepsis response and begin rapid fluid resuscitation per protocol.
Wait for the next chest x-ray before notifying anyone.
Offer oral fluids and repeat the blood pressure in an hour.
Document the vital signs as an expected fever response.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, altered mental status, tachycardia, and elevated lactate are high-risk findings for sepsis with hypoperfusion. The nurse should escalate immediately and begin protocol-driven resuscitation. Waiting for imaging or repeating vitals without intervention delays treatment during a time-sensitive emergency.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Wait for the next chest x-ray before notifying anyone is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, when the bedside priority is driven by Hypotension, altered mental status, tachycardia. Activate the sepsis response and begin rapid fluid resuscitation per protocol better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Offer oral fluids and repeat the blood pressure in an hour is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, when the bedside priority is driven by Hypotension, altered mental status, tachycardia. It is less defensible because Activate the sepsis response and begin rapid fluid resuscitation per protocol targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: Document the vital signs as an expected fever response is not the best answer, without accounting for Hypotension, altered mental status, tachycardia. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so It is less defensible because Activate the sepsis response and begin rapid fluid resuscitation per protocol targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Sepsis clues plus hypotension and high lactate require immediate escalation and fluid resuscitation.

questions.stem: A client admitted with an acute ischemic stroke asks for water because the mouth feels dry. Which action by the nurse is best?

options.text: Keep the client NPO until a swallow screen is completed.
Offer small sips of water while the nurse watches closely.
Give water with a straw to make swallowing easier.
Ask the family if the client has choked before.

questions.rationale: Clients with acute stroke are at risk for dysphagia and silent aspiration. The nurse should keep the client NPO until swallowing is assessed according to protocol. Even small amounts of water can lead to aspiration pneumonia if swallowing is impaired.

distractorRationales.b: Offer small sips of water while the nurse watches closely fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in A client admitted with an acute ischemic stroke asks for water because the mouth feels dry. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The nurse should prioritize Keep the client NPO until a swallow screen is completed; otherwise neurologic deterioration remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Give water with a straw to make swallowing easier the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by After stroke, do not give oral intake until swallowing safety has been checked. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Keep the client NPO until a swallow screen is completed better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Ask the family if the client has choked before the focus, without accounting for After stroke, do not give oral intake until swallowing safety has been checked. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Keep the client NPO until a swallow screen is completed is safer because it addresses neurologic deterioration before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: After stroke, do not give oral intake until swallowing safety has been checked.

questions.stem: A client admitted with suicidal ideation is placed on one-to-one observation. Which item should the nurse remove from the client's room first?

options.text: A drawstring hoodie.
A paperback book.
Non-skid socks.
A plastic water cup.

questions.rationale: Items that can be used for self-harm, including cords and strings, should be removed immediately from the environment of a suicidal client. A paperback book or water cup may be permitted depending on policy if they do not create a ligature or sharp-object risk. One-to-one observation does not replace environmental safety.

It is less defensible because A drawstring hoodie targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first. Nursing safety depends on matching the option to the client's active clinical risk.

questions.stem: A client prescribed isoniazid for tuberculosis asks what to watch for at home. Which symptom should the nurse instruct the client to report immediately?

options.text: Numbness or tingling in the hands or feet.
Orange discoloration of tears and urine.
A mild metallic taste after the dose.
An increased appetite in the evening.

questions.rationale: Isoniazid can cause peripheral neuropathy, especially in clients with nutritional risk factors, so numbness or tingling should be reported. Orange body fluid discoloration is expected with rifampin rather than isoniazid. Good teaching helps the client distinguish expected effects from adverse findings that need intervention.

Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of neurologic deterioration and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: An increased appetite in the evening would not address the priority, without accounting for Orange body fluid discoloration is expected with rifampin rather than isoniazid. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Numbness or tingling in the hands or feet better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Teach clients taking isoniazid to report peripheral neuropathy symptoms early.

questions.stem: A client newly prescribed warfarin asks which finding should be reported to the provider immediately. Which response by the client shows correct understanding?

options.text: I should report black, tarry stools.
I should expect my gums to bleed a little when brushing.
I can skip INR checks if I feel well.
I should double the next dose if I miss one.

questions.rationale: Black, tarry stools can signal gastrointestinal bleeding, which is a major complication of warfarin therapy and requires prompt evaluation. Minor bleeding should not be normalized, INR monitoring remains essential, and missed doses should never be doubled without direction. Teaching should focus on early recognition of serious bleeding and safe anticoagulant use.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with I should expect my gums to bleed a little when brushing is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, instead of responding to Black, tarry stools can signal gastrointestinal bleeding, which is a major complication of warfarin therapy and requires prompt. That mismatch risks slower recognition of bleeding or anticoagulation injury and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on I can skip INR checks if I feel well, instead of responding to Teaching should focus on early recognition of serious bleeding and safe anticoagulant use. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is I should report black, tarry stools, not a step that delays stabilization of bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in I should double the next dose if I miss one is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for Minor bleeding should not be normalized, INR monitoring remains essential. I should report black, tarry stools is safer because it addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Warfarin teaching should emphasize immediate reporting of serious bleeding signs such as black, tarry stools.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster would be unsafe, instead of responding to c diff spore precautions infection. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The nurse should prioritize Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Place the client on droplet precautions only, even though the higher-risk finding is c diff spore precautions infection. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel would be unsafe, despite the clinical warning in c diff spore precautions infection. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The nurse should prioritize Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort, but the stem cue is Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures, while the decisive cue is Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing because delaying infection or sepsis escalation can worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions is not the best answer, but the stem cue is Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing is safer because it addresses infection progression before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.

questions.stem: A toddler with croup has a barky cough and now has inspiratory stridor at rest. Which finding most concerns the nurse?

options.text: Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing.
Hoarse cry that improves when the child rests.
A barking cough that occurs mainly at night.
Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C).

questions.rationale: Stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness alone. The nurse should recognize this as a potential respiratory emergency because increasing work of breathing can quickly progress to fatigue and failure. Mild fever is common and not the most urgent finding here.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on Hoarse cry that improves when the child rests, rather than acting on In croup, stridor at rest is the red-flag sign of worsening airway obstruction. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: A barking cough that occurs mainly at night would not address the priority, when the bedside priority is driven by The nurse should recognize this as a potential respiratory emergency because increasing work of breathing can quickly progress. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C) the focus, and misses that The nurse should recognize this as a potential respiratory emergency because increasing work of breathing can quickly progress. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so It is less defensible because Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: In croup, stridor at rest is the red-flag sign of worsening airway obstruction.

questions.stem: An older adult who was oriented this morning is now disorganized, trying to climb out of bed, and seeing insects on the wall. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client is showing acute delirium that needs immediate assessment.
This is expected sundowning and can wait until morning.
The client is demonstrating stable chronic dementia.
This behavior is best managed by limiting all stimulation and delaying assessment.

questions.rationale: A rapid change in mental status with agitation and visual hallucinations is more consistent with delirium than chronic dementia. Delirium can be triggered by infection, hypoxia, medications, or metabolic problems and requires prompt assessment for reversible causes. Delaying evaluation increases the risk that a treatable medical problem will be missed.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with This is expected sundowning and can wait until morning is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, instead of responding to Delirium can be triggered by infection, hypoxia, medications, or metabolic problems and requires prompt assessment for reversible causes. That mismatch risks slower recognition of infection progression and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: Selecting The client is demonstrating stable chronic dementia would be unsafe, instead of responding to Sudden confusion and hallucinations in an older adult point to delirium until proven otherwise. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is The client is showing acute delirium that needs immediate assessment because delaying infection or sepsis escalation can worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward This behavior is best managed by limiting all stimulation and delaying assessment, and misses that Sudden confusion and hallucinations in an older adult point to delirium until proven otherwise. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The client is showing acute delirium that needs immediate assessment better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Sudden confusion and hallucinations in an older adult point to delirium until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A client taking lithium for bipolar disorder has vomiting, coarse hand tremors, and increasing confusion after several days of diarrhea. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the lithium and notify the provider.
Give the lithium with crackers to reduce stomach upset.
Teach the client to double the next dose.
Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further.

questions.rationale: Vomiting, worsening tremor, and confusion after fluid loss suggest lithium toxicity. The nurse should stop further doses and notify the provider for prompt evaluation. Dehydration and sodium loss can raise lithium levels, so more restriction or additional dosing would be unsafe.

distractorRationales.b: Give the lithium with crackers to reduce stomach upset fits a lower-risk interpretation, although the nurse should be guided by A client taking lithium for bipolar disorder has vomiting, coarse hand tremors. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The nurse should prioritize Hold the lithium and notify the provider; otherwise neurologic deterioration remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats Teach the client to double the next dose as the key issue, although the nurse should be guided by confusion after fluid loss suggest lithium toxicity. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so The safer answer is Hold the lithium and notify the provider because delaying neurologic safety assessment can worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further, although the nurse should be guided by A client taking lithium for bipolar disorder has vomiting, coarse hand tremors. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of neurologic deterioration and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: GI loss plus confusion and coarse tremor on lithium should be treated as toxicity until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: The RN is assigning care for a group of postoperative clients. Which task is appropriate to delegate to an experienced LPN/LVN?

options.text: Reinforce teaching already provided about incentive spirometry.
Perform the initial assessment of a client returning from PACU.
Develop the nursing care plan for a new admission.
Interpret a change in the client's telemetry rhythm.

questions.rationale: An experienced LPN or LVN may reinforce established teaching and perform focused care for stable clients, depending on policy. The initial postoperative assessment, care planning, and rhythm interpretation require RN-level assessment and clinical judgment. Safe delegation depends on stability, predictability, and the need for judgment.

distractorRationales.b: Perform the initial assessment of a client returning from PACU fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on Delegate stable, predictable care and reinforcement to the LPN or LVN, not initial assessment or interpretation. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The priority is Reinforce teaching already provided about incentive spirometry, not a step that delays stabilization of clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Develop the nursing care plan for a new admission is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, although the nurse should be guided by Delegate stable, predictable care and reinforcement to the LPN or LVN, not initial assessment or interpretation. That mismatch risks slower recognition of clinical safety risk and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Interpret a change in the client's telemetry rhythm, rather than acting on Safe delegation depends on stability, predictability. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of clinical safety risk and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Delegate stable, predictable care and reinforcement to the LPN or LVN, not initial assessment or interpretation.

questions.stem: A client is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis. Which infection-control measure should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Initiate droplet precautions.
Place the client in airborne isolation.
Use reverse isolation to protect the client.
Delay precautions until a lumbar puncture confirms the diagnosis.

questions.rationale: Suspected bacterial meningitis requires prompt droplet precautions to reduce transmission risk. The nurse should not wait for confirmation before initiating appropriate isolation. Airborne or reverse isolation do not match the primary transmission risk for typical bacterial meningitis.

distractorRationales.b: Place the client in airborne isolation is not the best answer, but the stem cue is Suspected bacterial meningitis requires prompt droplet precautions to reduce transmission risk. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Initiate droplet precautions, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Use reverse isolation to protect the client the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is Start droplet precautions right away when bacterial meningitis is suspected. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Initiate droplet precautions is safer because it addresses infection progression before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Delay precautions until a lumbar puncture confirms the diagnosis, while the decisive cue is Suspected bacterial meningitis requires prompt droplet precautions to reduce transmission risk. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Initiate droplet precautions because delaying infection or sepsis escalation can worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Start droplet precautions right away when bacterial meningitis is suspected.

questions.stem: A client receiving chemotherapy has an absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options.text: Avoid fresh flowers and raw fruits brought from outside.
Take a rectal temperature every 4 hours.
Limit oral care to once each shift to avoid bleeding.
Encourage the client to visit the crowded day room to reduce isolation.

questions.rationale: An absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3 indicates severe neutropenia and a high risk for infection. Reducing exposure to environmental organisms, including flowers and unwashed fresh produce, is appropriate. Rectal temperatures and unnecessary crowd exposure increase infection risk rather than protecting the client.

distractorRationales.b: Take a rectal temperature every 4 hours is not the best answer, but the stem cue is Rectal temperatures and unnecessary crowd exposure increase infection risk rather than protecting the client. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Avoid fresh flowers and raw fruits brought from outside is safer because it addresses infection progression before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Limit oral care to once each shift to avoid bleeding, instead of responding to An absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3 indicates severe neutropenia and a high risk for infection. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The safer answer is Avoid fresh flowers and raw fruits brought from outside because delaying infection or sepsis escalation can worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting Encourage the client to visit the crowded day room to reduce isolation would be unsafe, although the nurse should be guided by An absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3 indicates severe neutropenia and a high risk for infection. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Avoid fresh flowers and raw fruits brought from outside; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: With severe neutropenia, the nurse should lower exposure to infection sources rather than increase invasive or crowd contact.
